questions.prompt: What is this component?
questions.answer: LED (light-emitting diode)

questions.prompt: What is this component?
questions.answer: Resistor

questions.prompt: What is this component? (the tall cylinder with a stripe down one side)
questions.answer: Electrolytic capacitor

questions.prompt: What is this component? (the small disc with two leads)
questions.answer: Ceramic capacitor

questions.prompt: What is this component? (3 leads, flat black plastic body, rounded back)
questions.answer: Transistor (NPN, in a TO-92 package — the kind in our parts kit)

questions.prompt: What is this component?
questions.answer: Potentiometer (a knob whose position changes resistance — a 'pot')

questions.prompt: What is this component?
questions.answer: Push button (a momentary switch)

questions.prompt: What is this component?
questions.answer: Slide switch

questions.prompt: What is this thing called?
questions.answer: Breadboard

questions.prompt: What is this component called?
questions.answer: Seven-segment display (a 7-segment LED display)

questions.prompt: What is this chip called? (look at the number printed on it)
questions.answer: 555 timer

questions.prompt: Chips with numbers like 7400, 7408, 7432 belong to which family of chips?
questions.answer: The 74xx (or 7400) logic family — chips full of logic gates (AND, OR, NAND, etc.)

questions.prompt: What is this tool used for?
questions.answer: Stripping the plastic insulation off the end of a wire (wire strippers)

questions.prompt: What is this tool called?
questions.answer: Needle-nose pliers

questions.prompt: What is this tool used for?
questions.answer: Cutting wires (wire cutters / flush cutters)

questions.prompt: What does this schematic symbol represent?
questions.answer: Resistor

questions.prompt: What does this schematic symbol represent?
questions.answer: LED (the arrows mean it gives off light)

questions.prompt: What does this schematic symbol represent?
questions.answer: Capacitor

questions.prompt: What does this schematic symbol represent?
questions.answer: Battery (or a single cell — long line is +, short line is −)

questions.prompt: What does this schematic symbol represent?
questions.answer: A switch (SPST — single pole, single throw)

questions.prompt: This is the truth table for which gate?
questions.answer: AND

questions.prompt: This is the truth table for which gate?
questions.answer: OR

questions.prompt: This is the truth table for which gate?
questions.answer: NOT (also called an inverter)

questions.prompt: This is the truth table for which gate?
questions.answer: NAND (NOT-AND — it's AND, but flipped)

questions.prompt: What gate is this symbol?
questions.answer: AND (D-shape with a flat back)

questions.prompt: What gate is this symbol?
questions.answer: OR (curved back, pointed front)

questions.prompt: A resistor has bands: orange, orange, brown. What is its resistance?
questions.answer: 330 Ω (3, 3, ×10 → 33 with one zero added = 330 ohms)

questions.prompt: A resistor has bands: brown, black, red. What is its resistance?
A resistor has bands: brown, black, orange. What is its resistance?
On a standard breadboard, the long rails along each long edge (often marked red and blue/black) are usually used for what?
questions.answer: Power and ground (the power rails / bus strips — one rail is + and the other is −/ground)

questions.prompt: Look at the highlighted holes on this breadboard. Are these holes electrically connected to each other?
questions.answer: Yes — the five holes labeled A through E (or F through J) in a single numbered row are all connected together inside the breadboard

questions.prompt: Look at the highlighted holes on this breadboard. Are these holes electrically connected to each other?
questions.answer: No — these holes are in different numbered rows. Each numbered row is its own group of 5; they are NOT connected to neighboring rows in the central area

questions.prompt: Which of these two wirings is a valid 3-way switch circuit, A or B?
questions.answer: B is correct — each 3-way switch has one common terminal and two travelers; the travelers run between the two switches and the load (light) sits between the two commons. A is wrong because the travelers don't cross between the switches, so flipping one switch can't always change the light's state.

questions.prompt: Look at the table. The 4-bit binary for 4 is missing. What is it?
What is the decimal value of the 4-bit binary number 0110?
Does the LONG leg of an LED go to positive (power) or negative (ground)?
questions.answer: Positive — the long leg is the anode (+). The short leg is the cathode (−) and goes to ground.

questions.prompt: On an electrolytic capacitor (the tall cylinder kind), which side is marked with a stripe — positive or negative?
questions.answer: Negative — the stripe (and the shorter lead) marks the negative side

questions.prompt: Which leg of an LED is the cathode — the long leg or the short leg?
questions.answer: The short leg is the cathode (it goes to ground / the negative side)

questions.prompt: Which tool has notched / stepped jaws so you can squeeze and slide off the plastic insulation without cutting the metal wire underneath?
questions.answer: Wire strippers

questions.prompt: Which tool's pointy tips are best for grabbing tiny component leads or pulling parts out of tight spots on a breadboard?
questions.answer: Needle-nose pliers

questions.prompt: What is the morse code for S.O.S.?
questions.answer: ... --- ...   (three dots, three dashes, three dots — S is dot-dot-dot, O is dash-dash-dash)

questions.prompt: How many pins are on the Raspberry Pi Pico 2 W?
questions.answer: 40 pins (20 on each long side)

questions.prompt: What does the 'W' in 'Pico 2 W' stand for?
questions.answer: Wireless — it has built-in Wi-Fi (and Bluetooth)

questions.prompt: On a black DIP chip, what is the little notch / divot at one end for?
questions.answer: It marks the orientation of the chip. With the notch on the left, pin 1 is the pin just below the notch (bottom-left). Pin numbers count UP going counter-clockwise from the notch.

questions.prompt: Look at the chip — the notch is on the left, and the arrow is pointing to a pin. What pin number is this?
questions.answer: Pin 8 — the top-left pin (just above the notch). Pin numbers count up counter-clockwise from the notch, so pin 1 is the bottom-left pin and pin 8 is the last pin before wrapping back around.

questions.prompt: Once you've found pin 1 on a DIP chip, which way do the pin numbers go around the chip?
questions.answer: Counter-clockwise around the chip (when viewed from above). With the notch on the left, that means: across the bottom from left to right, up the right side, and back across the top.

questions.prompt: The 555 timer chip is famous for doing what?
questions.answer: Generating timing pulses or oscillating (blinking) signals — it's a timer / pulse generator

questions.prompt: Chips in the 74xx family (like 7400, 7408, 7432) contain what kind of circuits inside?
questions.answer: Logic gates (AND, OR, NAND, NOR, XOR, etc., packaged as little ICs)

questions.prompt: When you put two resistors in SERIES (one after the other), does the total resistance go up or down?
questions.answer: Up — resistances in series add together

questions.prompt: What is the main job of a resistor in a circuit?
questions.answer: To limit current (and/or drop voltage) — for example, to protect an LED from too much current

questions.prompt: A push button is called a 'momentary' switch. What does momentary mean?
questions.answer: It only connects the circuit while you're pressing it — it springs back open as soon as you let go

questions.prompt: What does 'normally open' mean for a button or switch?
questions.answer: When the button is NOT being pressed (its normal/resting state), the contacts are open — no current can flow until you press it

questions.prompt: What does LED stand for?
questions.answer: Light-Emitting Diode

questions.prompt: What does a capacitor do in a circuit?
questions.answer: It stores electrical charge (and releases it later) — like a tiny rechargeable battery for very short bursts

questions.prompt: What unit is resistance measured in?
questions.answer: Ohms (the symbol is Ω, the Greek letter omega)

questions.prompt: What unit is voltage measured in?
questions.answer: Volts (V)

questions.prompt: What unit is current measured in?
questions.answer: Amperes — usually shortened to amps (A)

questions.prompt: True or false: a diode lets current flow in only one direction.
questions.answer: True — that's the defining feature of a diode (it's a one-way valve for current)

questions.prompt: What voltage did we always set our battery packs to when powering our breadboards?
questions.answer: 5V (5 volts)

questions.prompt: If you connect an LED directly to a battery without a resistor, what is likely to happen?
questions.answer: The LED will burn out — it pulls too much current and dies. That's why we always put a current-limiting resistor in series with an LED.
